blurb: Classic large-flowered climber — sheets of velvety violet-purple on a sunny trellis.
sun: Full sun–part shade — top in sun, roots shaded
water: Moderate, consistent; keep roots cool & mulched
spread: Twines by leaf-stalks; clumping crown — not invasive/running
seasons: Showy blooms (spring or summer by pruning group); some give fluffy silver seedheads into fall/winter
toxic: Irritant/toxic if eaten; sap can irritate skin — caution with kids/pets. AVOID Clematis orientalis (yellow orange-peel clematis) — a CO List B noxious weed (all subspecies & cultivars)
origin: Garden hybrid — Chinese (C. lanuginosa) × S.-European (C. viticella) parents
habitat: Derived from woodland margins and rocky slopes in China and S. Europe

care.hardiness: Hardy to USDA zone 4 (RHS H6, to about -20°C) — well within the Front Range's zone 5b–6a. 'Jackmanii' and its large-flowered relatives are among the toughest clematis for Colorado; they die back to the ground in hard winters but reliably regrow from the crown each spring. This is not a problem — it's the expected and desirable behavior for a vine pruned by the Group 3 method (see 'maintenance').[1][2]
care.sun: Best in full sun to part shade, with 6+ hours of direct light for good flowering. In practice, the classic rule applies on the Front Range: sun on the top (the flowering stems), shade on the roots. Afternoon shade from a wall, low shrubs, or a layer of pebbles and mulch at the base keeps the root zone cool and moist, which clematis demand.[2][3]
care.soil: Moist but well-drained, moderately fertile soil — adapts to clay, loam, chalk, or sand provided it doesn't waterlog. On Colorado's alkaline clay-loam, work in generous compost at planting to improve drainage and moisture retention. Clematis are not fussy about pH (tolerate acid to alkaline) but hate standing water, especially in winter.[2][3]
care.water: Moderate and consistent — keep the root zone evenly moist, never waterlogged. Deep, infrequent watering (every 5–7 days in summer heat) is better than frequent shallow watering. Mulch generously (3–4 in of wood chips or gravel) to retain moisture and buffer soil temperature; pull mulch away from the crown to prevent rot. Newly planted vines need more frequent water until established.[3]
care.spacing: Space plants 18–24 in from a wall or fence, allowing airflow and room to root outward. Mature Group 3 vines reach 8–12 ft (2.5–4 m) tall with a 2–3 ft spread. Allow at least 3 ft between plants on a shared support; one plant can cover a 6–8 ft trellis panel. Provide a trellis, wire framework, or open shrub to twine through — clematis grip by leaf-stalks, not suckers.[2][3]
care.depth: Plant the crown 2–3 in (5–8 cm) deeper than it sat in its nursery pot. This deep planting is critical for clematis: if the vine develops clematis wilt (a fungal wilt that blackens and kills stems suddenly), buried buds below the soil surface survive and regenerate new growth. Without deep planting, wilt can kill the whole plant. Water in well at planting.[1][3]
care.bloom: Late June through September on the Front Range, with the main flush in July–August on current season's growth. Spent flowers may develop wispy silver-grey seedheads that persist into fall and add texture. Some cultivars in this group can give a lighter second flush after a late-spring cutback.[1][2]
care.feeding: Feed in spring as growth resumes with a balanced, slow-release fertilizer or a top-dressing of compost. A second application in early summer supports the long bloom season. Avoid heavy nitrogen, which promotes leafy growth over flowers. Mulch annually in spring.[3]
care.maintenance: As the vine grows, tie the stems evenly across the trellis or wires so it climbs as a sheet rather than a single bunch; a hard-pruned Group 3 vine can also be left to scramble up through a large deciduous shrub like lilac or Wood's rose (the annual prune is covered under 'pruning').[2]
care.troubles: Clematis wilt is the most alarming issue — stems suddenly blacken and collapse, sometimes overnight. Caused by a fungal pathogen; the solution is to cut the affected stems to the ground immediately and dispose of them (don't compost). Deep planting (see 'depth') provides underground buds that regrow. Aphids and earwigs may cluster on young shoots; check regularly. Scale, snails, and caterpillars are occasional visitors. In Colorado's dry air, fungal leaf diseases are rare. Avoid Clematis orientalis and all orange-peel/yellow clematis — they are CO List B noxious weeds.[3]
care.companions: Plant low, shade-giving companions at the base to keep roots cool: hardy geraniums, catmint, salvia, or lavender. Clematis looks spectacular paired with climbing roses (the violet blooms of 'Jackmanii' are a classic match for pink or cream roses) or scrambling through a large deciduous shrub like lilac or Wood's rose.[3]
care.planting: Plant container-grown clematis in spring (April–May) or early fall (late August–September) on the Front Range. Spring is the most practical window in Boulder: once the soil is workable and nighttime temperatures are consistently above 25°F (typically April), plant promptly so roots establish before summer heat. Fall planting by mid-September is also effective. Set the crown 2–3 in deeper than the nursery pot (see 'depth') and water in well. In containers, clematis can be grown but require close management: pot roots are far less cold-hardy than in-ground roots, so a zone-4 clematis in a pot may not survive a Front Range winter without moving the container into an unheated garage or insulated cold frame. Pots also dry out quickly — check moisture every day or two in summer; a Group 3 vine pruned hard each spring is the most manageable clematis for pot culture.[1][3]
care.propagation: Layering is the easiest reliable method for home gardeners: in spring or early summer, bend a flexible stem to the ground (or into a pot of compost set alongside), nick the underside of a node, peg it down, and cover with soil — roots form at the node within a few weeks to months; sever and transplant the rooted layer the following spring. Semi-ripe cuttings taken in June–July (4–6 in, nodal, with lower leaves removed) root well in a gritty mix under a humid cover at around 65–70°F, though they require a season to grow on before transplanting. Division of an established crown is possible but clematis resent root disturbance and divisions are slow to re-establish — layering or cuttings are preferable. Seed is rarely used for cultivars like 'Jackmanii' since offspring are variable and take 2–3 years to bloom. For home gardens, buying a container-grown plant is often the most practical starting point; propagate by layering once established.[3]
care.pruning: 'Jackmanii' is a Pruning Group 3 clematis — it flowers on the current season's new growth, so prune it hard every year in late winter or early spring (February–March on the Front Range), as soon as the lowest buds begin to swell.[1][2] Cut the whole plant down to a strong pair of buds about 12 in (30 cm) above the ground, removing all of the previous year's stems; this looks drastic but is exactly right for Group 3, since the vine regrows and flowers the same summer from the base.[1] In the first spring after planting, cut back just as hard to force several basal shoots and a well-clothed base.[1] (RHS notes 'Jackmanii' can alternatively be pruned as Group 2 — a lighter prune leaving a framework — for an earlier May–June flush on old wood plus a later one, but that needs more selective skill; Group 3 is simpler and recommended here.[1][2]) Skipping the hard prune, or treating it like an old-wood clematis, is the classic error: the vine turns into a bare tangle of dead-looking stems that flowers only in a knot at the very top.[1]

edible.summary: Garden clematis is poisonous — all parts are toxic if eaten and the sap irritates skin. No part is edible.[3][4]
edible.caution: Like other Clematis, 'Jackmanii' contains protoanemonin, an irritant compound released when the plant tissue is bruised or chewed. All parts are toxic — the leaves and sap are listed as the poison parts, and ingestion can cause intense burning of the mouth, severe mouth pain and ulcers, salivation, vomiting, and diarrhea; in larger amounts, dizziness, confusion, fainting, and convulsions are possible (severe poisoning is rare because the acrid, bitter taste discourages eating much). The same compound makes the fresh sap a skin and mucous-membrane irritant — handling cut stems can cause redness and a burning sensation, so wear gloves when pruning. It is toxic to dogs, cats, and horses as well as people; keep children and pets from chewing it. Note that drying largely destroys protoanemonin, but never rely on that — treat the whole plant as not for consumption.[3][4]
